title: Why Do Kids Get So Attached to One Specific Blanket?
published_at: TODO -- fill in before publish -- ISO datetime, set on actual publish
updated_at: TODO -- fill in before publish -- ISO datetime
summary: Psychoanalyst Donald Winnicott's transitional object theory explains why infants fixate on one specific blanket or teddy bear, and how developmental psychology and Richard Passman's security blanket experiments tested it.
answer: TODO -- fill in before publish -- one paragraph direct answer; can adapt from script.md's cold open / closing reframe
duration_iso: TODO -- fill in before publish -- e.g. PT8M30S, fill in once the final render duration is known
key_findings: Winnicott traced the origin of this even further back, to the infant's very first experience of the mother's breast.
There's a small, very concrete detail from Winnicott's clinical observations that captures how seriously this functions.
And it's worth being precise about what Winnicott himself said mattered here, because it's easy to get this backwards.
article_markdown: TODO -- fill in before publish -- fresh long-form written article for reading (NOT the spoken script verbatim). Use script.md and sources.json as source material.

sources.title: Winnicott, D.W. (1953). Transitional Objects and Transitional Phenomena: A Study of the First Not-Me Possession. International Journal of Psychoanalysis, 34, 89-97.
sources.publisher: Transitional object concept coined; typically emerges 4-12 months, manifesting as a blanket, cloth, or soft toy

sources.title: Winnicott, D.W. (1953). Transitional Objects and Transitional Phenomena: A Study of the First Not-Me Possession. International Journal of Psychoanalysis, 34, 89-97.
sources.publisher: 'First not-me possession' framing -- the object occupies a middle position between 'me' and 'not-me'

sources.title: Winnicott, D.W. (1953). Transitional Objects and Transitional Phenomena. International Journal of Psychoanalysis, 34, 89-97; further developed in Winnicott, D.W. (1971). Playing and Reality. Tavistock Publications / Basic Books.
sources.publisher: Origin traced to the infant's experience of the mother's breast; the 'moment of illusion' of infant omnipotence, later continued by the transitional object

sources.title: Winnicott, D.W. (1971). Playing and Reality. Tavistock Publications / Basic Books, p. xii.
sources.publisher: Direct quote -- 'not so much the object used as the use of the object,' clarifying the object itself isn't what matters, the function it serves is

sources.title: Passman, R.H. & Weisberg, P. (1975). Mothers and blankets as agents for promoting play and exploration by young children in a novel environment: The effects of social and nonsocial attachment objects. Developmental Psychology, 11(2), 170-177.
sources.publisher: Controlled experiment: 2-3 year olds in an unfamiliar playroom explored/played and showed as little distress with their own security blanket present as with their mother present; children given an unfamiliar toy or nothing explored/played less and were more distressed -- directly answers Beat 5's own hedge that no controlled experimental evidence exists for this topic

sources.title: Encyclopedia.com, "Transitional Phenomena" entry (summarizing Winnicott's original 1951/1953 formulation and explicitly correcting the common misapprehension that a transitional object is required for healthy development).
sources.publisher: Required hedge / correction -- Winnicott never claimed a transitional object is necessary for healthy development; this is a common misreading of the theory
